What's up? I hope you guys had a good lunch. All right. So just as an aside, for the first half of the presentation, it's going to be mostly myself talking. And then we will switch to Alex. Alex is in a peanut gallery right now, so just for observation. We call this one cheerleader versus fullback. It was actually a term I heard, which you can think what you want, and I have my own thoughts myself. But Gary Vee mentioned it one time, and I really liked the term cheerleader versus fullback in terms of partnerships and relationships, right? And so last year, our partnership presentation was a personal favorite of many who are still here today. And so we did want to make a note that if you have not watched it from last year, it might help you further. And so if you haven't, and you leave here thinking this was helpful for you today, we suggest that you go back and also watch last June's. But rather than do that again, we wanted to share with you all the basics that are needed, any partnership along with the two relationship dynamics that we see lead to the most success for entrepreneurs, business owners, et cetera. So I wanted to kind of explain what we see partnerships as being the foundation that we build our lives upon, right? And so to give you a visual, this is how we think of everything. We think of spiritual health and wellness, business, finances, romance, relationships, everything is relying on the partnership dynamic. So we think, okay, I'll give you an example, right? If Alex and I get into a tiff in the morning, right, before we were to start our day, if that were to happen for any reason, which probably maybe once a quarter does, then we will say we are not starting our day until we resolve this issue right here. Because we look at it like our partnership dynamic is more important than anything else. And it will ruin all the rest of the dynamics for the day if we don't fix what's happening between us, right? That's on a daily basis. So if you're thinking about, if you haven't talked to the partner that you have about something that's bugging you, and it's been months, like imagine how much more of that you're affecting, right? Therefore, we really think that choosing the right partner and then working your ass off can either set you up for success, failure, or somewhere in between, right? And so the two dynamics that we see that work for an entrepreneur that we'll share with you. Number one is fullback. So this is the dynamic in which your significant other is your chosen partner in battle, right? And so you literally pick this person to have your back if the army of the walking dead came and they were trying to kill you, right? You'd be like, oh, dude, I totally want Alex on my side, like picking him for my team, right? And that's how our dynamic is, right? Some other people that you see with this dynamic, you're like power couples, right? So what does this look like, right? Tactically. One, you share the same mission, right? Two, you have similar interests. You share the power equally. You intertwine your entire lives with one another. Many would say that this comes from a book that would talk about codependency, right? Work, hobbies, friends, etc. Oh my gosh, you guys rely on each other for everything, okay? But if you don't rely and trust someone else, right, with a lot of pieces of your life, just like you might with your business, then you might not be able to grow as quickly. And so that's just how we see it with each other, right? So for us, we have the same mission, bring the industry from its knees to its feet, change the game for everybody. Similar interests, we both like eating, working out, you know, he likes staying inside. I like going outside. There's a difference. And we share the power equally, meaning like when it comes to decisions, we don't move forward until we both agree on something, right? And then yes, in tining, in intertwining your entire lives, we like to have the same friends, things like that. Number two, cheerleader, right? So this dynamic is one in which your partner may not be the one in the battlefield with you, but they are your number one supporter who without, you would never take the insane risks you do. And so what I want you to think about is like, you're the entrepreneur jumping off the building, and this person is that cushion that you're going to fall on, right? And so you feel okay taking those risks because that person is supporting you so heavily, right? And you know that they're not going to beat you up at the end of the day if you mess up because they support you. Can I add a quick question? Who here thinks that they have a fullback dynamic in their relationship? Wow, okay, it was a lot less than I thought it was going to be. So who here thinks they have more of a cheerleader dynamic? Okay, cool. That was just really good to hear. Sorry, go ahead. Sweet. So what does the cheerleader dynamic look like, right? This person is your number one fan who attends every game. The cheerleader creates a safe place where the other spouse can recharge. They may have different interests than you, but they encourage each other in them, right? So you're like, she likes doing that. I like doing this and like, you don't do them together, but you encourage each other to pursue those interests. And one might be the bread maker while the other has a more casual approach to life. We hear that a lot. It's like someone's the one who's super money driven, super like purpose driven, the other one's like more casual, like I just want to enjoy relationships and the people around me, right? Note, and this is really important because we read all the feedback that you guys give us and that you ask about relationships. The cheerleader understands and respects your love of the game would never ask you to sit out unless you were her, right? So that person should never ask you to sit out the games unless they're like, bro, you have a broken ankle and you've been playing on it and it's freaking swollen. Like you need to sit out, right? Some other dynamics in which you see this, which there was a picture there and it's gone. His wife is not ex-wife. He has a wife. Yeah, I mean, there's only one picture on the internet, right? Some other dynamics, right? Cheerleader. So all that being said, can you identify which relationship you are in, right? Because this is the truth about relationships. Is that regardless of whichever dynamic you fit into, neither will work if there's not an equal exchange of value, okay? So this sounds very logical because it is and it is taking and stripping the emotion out of what a relationship is. But how we look at it, spouse one gives xxx to spouse two. Spouse two gives xxx to spouse one, right? And they just keep giving to each other. And so say spouse one gives financial support, family status and security. Spouse number two may give unwavering support, takes care of the nest and affection, right? So you can give and take the same things to each other or different. The point is that you give and take equally, right? And that you know what you're giving and taking. You've established that with each other. And here's the secret. You will always feel like you give more than you take. So despite it needing to be equal from an absolute standpoint, you need to let go of everything that says this for that. And you have to make the decision to be the giver in the relationship. Because we're really, really, really bad at acknowledging what other people do for us. And we often overestimate what we do for others. Like, I can tell you how many times I think to myself, I'm like, compliment Alex more, compliment Alex more. And then I'm like, all right. And I literally write it down. I'm like, check it off. And I'm like, I did it three times today. And then I, and then I talked to him a couple days later, and he's like, it would really be cool if you compliment me more. I'm like, look, like there's so much. I'm like, man, I crossed my teeth and dodged my eyes all week. Like what? You know what I mean? So best case, both people go, both people go into it, trying to out give each other, right? Second best case, you go in trying to out give the other person, they don't reciprocate. Note, the action on your part is the same, which is literally the only thing you can control in your relationship. It's you giving your 100% to your 50%. Right. And that's what you should focus on. You shouldn't focus on what that person isn't doing for you. You focus on how do I give more, right? Because oftentimes what we see in someone else is what we're not doing, right? You see that they're not giving to you, because in reality, you know that you're not giving to them. But what happens without this perspective? Resentment, right? It's like the death of relationships, resenting your other ones. Here's the thing, is that most people and many people in this room resent your spouse for the things that they don't do that you haven't asked for. And if you don't ask, the answer is always no, right? Unexpressed expectations are the death of relationships, right? That being said, this is what we see, the foundation for successful relationships to be, whether you're dynamic number one or number two. One, oh, I hit puberty there, shared mission. You both agree on what is your goal as a unit in life, right? Do you want to create a legacy together? Do you want to have a family together? Do you want to drive world change? Do you want to inspire change in others? Like, what is it that you two together are working towards as a whole, right? Mind you, you can see that we're probably biased, because three out of those four are what a fullback dynamic would be. And if you had a cheerleader, dynamic, probably just the second one to have a family would be the scenario that you would have as your goal. So just something to consider. The other one might have been enjoy the fruit of the labor that you, you know, that's kind of the juxtaposition between the lifestyle business of wanting a business that can pay for the life that I want to have outside versus like, I want to have a purpose driven business that like cures cancer, right, or cures obesity in the area. That one will get a lot bigger than this one. Neither one's right or wrong. It's just what you actually want and being real about truly what you got in this for. Yeah. I mean, we know a lot of people who are in it for lifestyle, and so we meet them and they're, they don't want to grow their business, they just want to support their lifestyle. And that's fine. That's totally cool if you feel that way. Like, and if you all in this room feel that way, like there's nothing wrong with that at all, just a different goal. Aligned values. So these are the values that you use to make decisions regarding each of your lives, as well as your life together as a unit, right? Sound familiar like core values? Yeah, because ours are the same as our team values. So when we actually went together, we were like, all right, we're gonna make our core values for the business. We're like, these are how we're going to run our life too. Makes it super simple. Three, clear expectations. So you have clarity of roles as individuals in context of your relationship. Guys, this one's huge. Like, this is the one that most people don't do, because it involves writing something down on a piece of paper. So you have clarity of around roles as individuals. One, who makes the money? Who takes care of the house? Who takes care of the finances? Who takes care of the family? Who maintains outside relationships with friends? Right? Second, the wish list and cheat codes. And so this is actually what we did, and we had a legacy call where we talked about this. And I want to repeat it because a lot of you didn't see it, didn't hear it, or haven't looked at it, right? But it works really well because it gives you what we call an ideal scene. Okay, so what is the wish list or the cheat codes? In other words, it's writing out what each person wants from the other person, so that this is what it looks like when it's perfect, right? Like, when you have done everything that I was expecting of you, and then I do everything you're expecting of me. So, for example, I give you like five of each of us. What Alex would like from Layla? Daily shoulder rubs. Layla to patiently listen to all of Alex's ideas. Daily compliments as many as possible. Sex at night. Layla make all of Alex's food. What Layla would like from Alex? Daily walk together. Alex listened to Layla without offering solution. Tell Layla she's pretty and in shape. Sex in the morning. Alex thanked Layla for always, for the task she does for him. Okay? It's simple, but it's like having it written down, I take this and I put it on my whiteboard and sitting there staring at me all day every day, right? Do you think I'm going to do it? I'm going to pay attention to it since it's brought me all day? Probably. This, I was just going to piggyback on this. Like, being, I hope I don't jump ahead because I know we have some other slides about it, but you have, like, the only way that this is useful is if you don't try and write down what you think you should be writing down. I'll say it again. This is only going to be useful if you write down what you really want, not what you think you should want or what you think that person would, would increase your status and your relationship with that person of what you think that they want you to hear. I know that sounds crazy to say, but like, hopefully understand my point. Like, people are weird. You might like weird things. I'm not saying even sexually. I'm just saying like, you might just like weird shit. Like, really? You know what I mean? Like, I don't know. Like, maybe you like it when, you know, your spouse sings in the shower and like, like hearing that like really makes your day. I don't know. I just picked that out of like a hat, but I'm just saying like, that might be something that like means a lot to you, right? Or it might be the reverse, you know, like, never sing again. But, but the point is, is that like, typically like the ones that mean the most to us are the ones that like, give us like, in a public setting like this, the least status. So being like, why does Alex want to be complimented all the time? Probably because I have deep seated insecurities that are trying to fold success, right? So like, this would be real. So anyways, but like, you know what I mean? Like, just being real, like, I know that's not cool. It's not, you know what I mean? But it's what I what I want. You know what I mean? And when she cost me, I'm like, especially when she says something about something that I care the most about, like, so Layla's opinion of what I do means more to me than everyone in this room put together. And I don't say that as an affront to you, but as a compliment to her. Like, if she if she like listens to a podcast of mine, and she's like, that podcast today was awesome. I literally feel lighter. I'm like, nailed that podcast. I'm like, you guys hear that one? I was like, drop some nuggets just saying. But I feel amazing, but like, it's not cool. So like, try and think of like, what are the not cool things that I actually really want? Or another way to think about it, if you're really not like, good at knowing what you like, because you might have might not have thought through this, is some of you guys have heard of the five, five love languages. I'm sure a lot of you have. But thinking about it in terms of like, what verticals you receive and give love. So some people like gifts, like I neither of us care about like any physical like material goods at all, which is great because neither of us give or take gifts. We don't really celebrate holidays. It works out great. So but like for some people, you might not care at all. And your wife just wants you to like make a card and it would like make her month, right? Any ladies or any guys who would like get a card that would like mean anything. Thank you. One person. That's good. So exactly, right? So you've got gifts, you've got acts of service, like when Layla like makes me food, it means a lot to me. I think it's like, it's her serving me that like that's something that means a lot to me. Words of affection. So like compliments. So thinking about it in that, in that vein is another one that a lot of people fall into physical touch. It doesn't have to be sexual. So you can just be like, I just need a hug. So like, I know a lot of people who like, when they get really stressed, it's like, can you just hug me and just just hug me. Do it all the time. Keep going. And like that literally like, like babies don't mature right. Like you have biological feedback that is not like not psychological biological from hugs. So like, it's not necessarily even that person's control. It's not cool. It's not whatever. Just be true. Right. And then there's a fifth one, which is quality time, which Layla loves. And I don't care. But but like the daily walks for her sound like sweaty and I'm going to get thigh chafing. But it means a lot to her. So it's like, you know what I mean? Like if you think about it through those five silos, and then you actually make your perfect day, it becomes a cheat code. And then like life gets a lot easier when we did this in our marriage to like change everything. Because I was like, this is all I need to do. So all this other stuff that I thought you wanted, I'm good to not do that anymore. She was like, yeah, I was like, all right. I was like, just these ones. She was like, and I'm good. I was like, you're good. Okay. How's that podcast? Where can you focus your energy to get to maximize your ROI? Because if you don't know what that person wants, you're spending all your time on all these things that they might not even like. Right. And obviously, if you can't define this for yourself, then how can you expect the other person to guess, right? And if you do this with your spouse, like Alex said, if you give them a watered down version of what you want, then you guarantee a watered down version of what you want as best case, which 100% guarantees not getting what you want. And like Alex said, most people are afraid to ask for what they really want. And so they instead, they ask for what they think they should want, what they think is cool, right? Like Alex said, asking for compliments for me. It's like, tell me I'm pretty in in shape because I'm still that fat girl from like the picture you saw in the last slide, right? So like, I probably want him to tell me that because I still have deep seed insecurities as well. And it's like, that stuff's not cool, but it's the stuff that like, as humans, we need. I need to be secure with being vulnerable about the things you want from your spouse. Because the truth is, and we talk about this, like, if we can't share our authentic human experience with each other, who the fuck are we going to share with? Right? Like, I'm not going to pay a therapist to hear that. Like, he's my husband, right? And your partner, that's who you want to share it with. Trust. So people talk about like, what is trust? All stuff all. Trust is just built through consistently being honest with people, right? And sometimes brutally honest with people. And that was a step that we took in our relationship before we got married. We had stuff that we were like, all right, we feel like we're sugarcoating with each other. We felt like we weren't confronting issues that we had with each other. And so we sat down and we were like, I just remember being at the table. And we were like, let's just lay it all out. Let's just lay all our garbage out right now. And it was hard. And there were tears on my side. And it was not fun. But it was a moment. And if you need that moment going forward, then do that with your spouse, because it's just like, let's lay it all out right now. And then going forward, we're going to tell each other the truth about everything. What do I like? What do I not? Like, what's on my mind? What's bothering me? Like, any and everything, right? It's being honest about how you feel, honest about what you need from your spouse, and honest about what you were lacking from your spouse, right? One of the biggest things that we tell each other is like, what am I lacking from you right now? Like, what do I need from this relationship? Or when we talk each week, we talk once a week about like, how are we doing in our relationship? It's like, I feel like we really lacked here, right? Because people aren't perfect. And we just have to, if you're not measuring it in some way, which is typically just measuring through conversation, then you're not going to be able to track it when it dips a tongue, right? You're not going to be able to bring it back up quickly. Don't sugarcoat it, right? You're not doing your spouse a service, you're doing them a disservice by not telling them the truth. Because you're not giving them the tools to actually love you in a way that you want. If you think about it, like, imagine you have all this desire to care for this other person, and they do not give you the tools to love them the way that you want to love them, in the way that like, they feel most loved. If you don't have that tool, you would be really, really upset about the fact you're like, why don't you give it to me? And they're that person's like, because I'm afraid of what you think of me. You're like, I don't care. Like, you can, whatever you want, like, one cup of jelly all over you, like, okay, you know, I'm like, just tell me what you want. You know what I mean? Like, just if you think about it from the other person's perspective, I think sometimes that can help you break through in like, a conversation. Transparency. So, like, Tony Robbins says, you stay in your head, you're dead, right? I say that to myself quite often. So transparency, what is that? Sharing your true inner experience of life with one another, one another, despite fear of judgment, right? So it's like, when someone asks your spouse says, like, what's bothering you? And you're like, I'm fine. Or like, you're having a bad day, and then you sit down at the end of a bad day, and you're thinking to yourself, like, what bugged you? But you don't want to tell them because you're embarrassed. Or you don't want to tell them because you feel like you're embarrassed for yourself, you're judging yourself, right? Those are the things you need to share with your spouse, right? And so this is how we see the foundations or the pillars of the relationship, right? So if you look at it, you've got your shared mission. That's your foundation, drive the world forward, create a legacy for your family, etc., whatever it may be. Your aligned values. What values do you use to run your life? Your clear expectations? What do you do versus what do I do? Trust. Those are the pillars that then hold up the roof, and the transparency are the windows to see in. So that you can screenshot if you want. That's how we look at relationships. That being said. Our graphics team did not make that. If you build the foundation right, then you can add whatever else you want to it, right? So suddenly, if you've built that foundation, everything else, and we talk about help, finances, relationships with friends, all that stuff seems to explode, right? So it's like, you want a pool, you can add a pool to the house, you want to get a trampoline, you can add a trampoline to the house, you want to get a car, you can get a nice car, you want to have kids, you can have kids, right? Because you have the foundation solid. So that being said, we want to go into some rapid fire Q&A. So I'm going to let Alex take it over from here because we made a post. Some of you may have seen it a couple of weeks ago and we asked, you know, what questions do you have for us about relationships? What do you guys want to hear about with relationships? Because not just from our experience, but from what we see with other people that we're around who are in relationships that are running businesses. Okay. So just to piggyback on what Layla said at the very, very beginning, last summit here, when we gave a survey and asked what was your favorite presentation, it was the biggest surprise in the world to me, but the one that everyone said was that by far, the winner was the talk about the integrator and, well, actually we call it the first follower, but basically the entrepreneur and the operator, that first person who follows you into the battle, right? And that doesn't necessarily need to be your spouse. You can't like right now, some of you guys have an operations manager who's like your right hand, who's that person who believes in you, who helps you with everything, who you always feel indebted to because they work way harder than you, all that, right? Anyone? Is that ringing your bell to anyone? Okay. If you don't have that person and you're a soloprinter, you need to find that person as soon as possible. The last presentation details what that dynamic should look like, and then the reason that this presentation is just a little bit more romantically applied was because there were a lot of people that felt like we were just talking about the full back dynamic. And so we wanted to peel that back and talk about both equally and then address some of the questions that came up from the post I made the other day. Okay. So I bucketed all the questions into like five categories, I think. These were all the questions. A lot of them are kind of repetitive, just asked in different ways. And so what I did was I, that's all of your questions. And then I summarized them into bullets. Quality time. And so we're going to just go back and forth because I figured rather than having the cue box and sometimes it's hard to hear and blah, blah, blah. These are all the questions you guys already asked and we figured we'd answer them now. So quality time. How often, how long would it discuss how to keep romantic, how to keep romance not worked? You guys have a date night. So quality time is more Layla's thing, right, of the love language is like she's like spending more quality time together. And because of that, because I love my wife, we spend lots of time together. And so we spend every morning together, we drink coffee and that's usually like an hour long thing. And we usually just like chill. We chat about kind of what we're going to do for the day. A weird dream. I mean, we just like, we really just chill and drink our coffee. We both drink decaf, but like we just drink coffee because we like the process. We have lunch together. And as soon as we have dinner, actually as soon as our work days over, which for us is like 430-ish, that's when we work out. And pretty much as soon as we get to the gym, like not get to the gym, it's in our house, but like at 430 we stop. So we work out. And then after that, like we don't really work. That being said, we totally talk about work. I would say that the difference is we talk about more opportunities, wishful things, things that are like fun and exciting. We're not talking about decisions. Like what are we going to do about that member who needs a refund? What are we going to do about that employee who is late? Are we going to have the talk who wants to like, that's like dirt. Like we don't talk about dirt after a certain hour, but we're not going to stop talking about work because it's our mission. Both of us want to go there. And so we talk about what we want to talk about. I've just never seen anyone who has a spouse that works together like make that work, but being like, we can't talk about work. And then they just like look at each other like, how was your day? Good? Like, you know, like it just, it's just not natural. Doesn't make sense. And yes, we do two date nights a week. We do one date night that's like us and then one that we try and we committed to being more social because we don't really have friends. So we're like, we're going to go out once a week and we're like, you, you're a person. Come on out. And so we're doing that now. So we do that like once a week. Doing a piggyback on that. Not you got it. All right. Sweet. So intimacy, how do you keep it alive? I think a lot of that. So I think that's a lot of individuality. You know what I mean? Like, I don't need to get my intimacy alive. But Laila, it's more like catering to what Laila needs. You know, just test storm. Like, it's really, I think catering to like, usually there's probably one person that like give a gas and a break. So it's probably catering to like, what would make the break not break, you know, and then just going and doing those things. Like Laila likes talking. So we talk all the time. All right. So I think I think a lot of it for keeping intimacy alive. I think people need to get out of their own environments. Like really, for us, we committed, we like, we do not like taking vacations. I will say that. But we like getting out of our environment. So we like to leave and go physically to a different environment, continue to do a lot of the same stuff we would do if we were at home, but just being out of that environment and having a different routine and some variety. We start talking about things that we wouldn't be talking about if we were at home. And so we've actually tried to be scheduling them. So like this year for January, when New Year's start, I was like, all right, plan out the rest of the year. Like, here's when we'll take vacations. Here's the things we'll do. So vacations for us are like, oh, we have something for work and we'll extend it by two days in a day. Right. So for like some of you here, I would say if you have a summit to go to, stay a day before, stay a day after, make it like a date thing, right? Stuff like that. Or we'll do a staycation. We just like go and we used to do that a lot where we would stay somewhere that's 20 minutes from us. It's just a nice hotel. And you just go there and like, chill. Yeah, it's, that's like, I love staycations. Just just, I mean, you get rid of all the travel, like costs and pain in the ass, which is what we hate the most about it. But like, if you drive 40 minutes in one direction, you're probably not somewhere that you are currently. And you might as well be in Tulsa if you're from North Carolina, like it's just another area that you're not used to. And you get pretty much all the same benefits and you don't have to worry about anything. So we're big fans of that. Family, having kids. Yeah, that one, we don't have kids. And we sort of show it's not too for now, because we're focusing on trying to fix the gym community. But yeah, I'm sorry. I don't know. Yeah, sorry, we didn't have more people on that. But I can tell you we have some great people on our team who've got anywhere from four to five kids and they still freaking crush it and have great marriages. So definitely know it's possible. Wishlist, what happens when one person doesn't fulfill the other person's wants needs? That's not under your control. It's, I mean, first, you have to not resent the person. You need to communicate it. But it'll like, and this is where there's the like, you can you can say something that's true and be a dick. You can say something that's true and loving. It's it really depends on where you're saying it from. That makes sense. I know that's like, the worst and like cheesy or like softest answer ever. But just saying like, Hey, when you did this, or you didn't do this, I was I like really needed that today. Like, do you think maybe, can you say that right now? Really? Could you listen to my podcast right now and tell me what you think? But you know what I mean? And then like, do you think you could do that tomorrow? And I mean, most people were like, Oh, crap, like they'll set an alarm and or she'll set an alarm and she'll do it the next day. And I probably messed up more than she really ever does. Yeah, I think a lot of the times we don't want to tell someone how they made us feel because there's no reason behind it in relationships. A lot of times it's illogical, or it has to do with something that might be wrong with you. So like, maybe I, Alex said something that made me feel a certain way. And I'm like, but it's tied to this. That's my issue. But it's our relationship. And so I'm like, Hey, I'm working on this. But when you say that, it makes me feel this way. Can you help me out and say it like this instead? Yeah, even if I'm wrong, you know, I mean, like, I shouldn't ask this of her. But I'm just going to say like, I know that this is a thing I'm working on. If you could not say good boy, that was actually something that like really bugged me for like, whatever reason, like when someone says like, Good boy, I'm like, That just really bugs me. I don't know why it just does just feels really disrespectful. Really, it feels like you're talking to a dog. I don't like it. So anyways, that's the point. It's like she said something like earlier. I was like, That's something that really bugs me. I know you didn't say it in a mean way. But like, if you could not say it while I figure out how I not be angry about it, that would be great. But like just stuff like that. Celebration. How do you celebrate big and like just for the wish that like you can't get mad at someone. Like you just said, it's like a relationship. Like you have to just keep you coach people to success just like an employee, you know, celebration. How do you celebrate big achievements? You want to take this one? We don't. We honestly, we were talking about this, we celebrate progress more than like big achievements. So it's like, like, it's so awesome that like, you've been working on this. And like, I've really noticed that like, you've been way more empathetic towards these people on the team, or like, you've really been like asking me or giving me these compliments more often, blah, blah. I mean, like, I don't know, I really view it like with any relationship or any dynamic of your life. It's like the small little things that add up to the big win. Right. And so it's like, instead of celebrating big achievements, I'd rather celebrate like development of character, or like things like that. Like we point out those things and compliment each other on those things and talk about those things as as success a lot of the times. And so by the time you hit the goal, you already knew you were going to hit it anyway. So it's like, we're not going to fake it. You're like, wow, right? Because we knew we were going to hit it like a month before, right? Yeah, we're more pumped about like your deadlift form looks a lot better than but like once you're hitting your pool 485 hitting 500, just doesn't like it's not that cool. But like seeing the form improve is like what we more celebrate. We don't do that consciously. It's just something we both noticed. How do you do this? If the other person doesn't understand, so I think this is a larger context like let's say you hit a record month is your first six figure month, right? And you're like, holy shit, I just hit six figures like this is amazing. This is like I've been shooting for this for 10 years. Like this is massive, right? And then you get home and you're like, honey, we did 100 grand this month. And she's like, where are the bacon? You know, like, where's it at? I don't see no 100 grand. Like, well, there's overhead. There's other things that go into this. Like how do you like how do you how do you do that? I think it's 100% being vulnerable about like how much this means to you. You know what I mean? And I think it's usually if someone lacks context, they don't understand. Like if someone says like we hit a hundred grand this month, it's like, is that good? Is that a lot? I don't know. You know what I mean? Like, is it? I mean, it sounds like a lot of money. I you know, whatever. But on the flip side, if like, I'm using a girl example for me because I wouldn't understand it. But if it was like, oh, God, I just got invited to be, I was gonna be president of the PTA, you would never do that. But like, I just got president of the PTA. But I knew that it had been something that she'd always aspired to and her mom was part of the PTA. And like she'd been working towards it. And she was doing all these volunteering things. And she brought the Jello cake that she loves. And she looked at the recipe all day. You know what I mean? If you see the work and you understand the context of it, maybe like, that's amazing, maybe like, let's go out and celebrate. But like, I think a lot of times we're expecting someone to have context to a situation they don't know about. Nothing else. Honestly, like, just ask, like, if Alex is like, I would like you to tell me, like, that's freaking awesome when my biceps are 20, whatever inches, you know, then I'm gonna remember and be like, Alex, that's awesome, your biceps are so big, right? Like, does it make a difference at this point for me? No. But like, it makes a difference to him and our relationship if I celebrate it with him, right? So like, if anything, don't, I don't think that the cheerleader needs to understand the goal as much as they need to understand that celebrating it means a lot to the other person. Right? Pressure, how do you not resent your partner for I think you you had a great answer for this. So how do you not resent your partner for not understanding the pressure? They didn't choose to be put in a position of pressure. Like you chose to own a business. And if that person chose not to, you can't resent them for that because you made the choice to have the business like you're not a victim of the business, right? So like, you can't resent your spouse for not feeling the pressure because they chose not to and you chose to. Simple as that. Yeah, it's all I got on that one. Oh, how do you make your how do you make business pull you together rather than pull you apart? I'm just gonna go to the next one. Like Mimi. This takes a lot of thought. I mean, I think it was I don't even know I tried to include all of them. I'm just gonna go to the next one. Okay, so decision making agreements and disagreements. Okay, so big ideas. How do you communicate about big ideas without hurting the other person's feelings? So this was in the context, if I remember properly, this is coming from the operator. This is coming from the integrator. This is coming from the first follower talking about how do you talk to the entrepreneur about like, hey, I know you have all these great ideas, but like our house is on fire, we should probably do with that first. So do you want to? Yeah, I think a lot of it is timing. So like, as someone like Alex always has tons of big ideas, he always wants to tell me about them. And so I'm like, okay, cool. One thing is I make myself very aware of how I'm reacting to it, because I think it I never want to make him feel bad for having ideas, because that's what drives the business forward. Right. And so if you're the operator, like, you never want to feel bad about that. You never want them to feel bad about that for one. For two, a lot of times it's like, is this the right time to talk about it? And that goes for both of us. So is this the right time to tell Layla all your big ideas when she's in the middle of meetings? Or is it the right time to tell Alex when he's super excited that you don't want to hear it and that there's all this other shit going on? We can't do that. Right. It's like timing thing. So sometimes I think listen, and then say, cool, like, I'm, that's great. And like, let's talk about this later. And then think of how you want to deliver the feedback to them. Right. Because like, if you are heated in the moment, because a lot of times what happens is like the visionary is like communicating the operator, and the operator is like, oh my God, I can't take this right now. Like, there's so much shit going on. I just got three cancellations over here. You don't even know. Right. And so you're like, and it's like, instead, just take a break. You're like, okay, we're going to talk about this later. Like, let's talk about this after our day's done. And then talk about it then and then approach it with like, okay, here's your idea. Here's the things we have going on right now. Just very logically, like, is it the best decision for the business? So everything that she just said makes sense. That drives me up a wall. So what I've learned, being the one who has the big ideas, is that it's a lot easier for me to have the big idea and like control it than to go there excited with the likelihood that I get turned out. Because I've learned that now. Because she's usually on a meeting, hasn't had her coffee yet, or a number of other reasons where she's not like ready to take that. And so what I've learned to do is I'll have the idea, I'll chew on it for a second, and then I'll box or I'll shoot her a text or something and just be like, are you in a state to be able to hear something right now? Like, seriously, like, I will ask that. And then if she's like, yeah, I'm free, like shoot. Then it's like, all right. So it's like, I got this idea. Right. But if she doesn't, then it has been so much easier for me from an emotional level to ask permission first with, because it's like, I'm not going, I'm not being like, I got to tell you about this. And she's like, I'm busy. And I'm like, you will be, you know, like, like this changes everything. You don't need to be on meetings. Like this could change the whole business. You know, we won't need meetings anymore. I'm kidding. But like for real, though, so for those of you who have the ideas, which is probably most of the people in this room, like I have not figured out any hack on their side. It has been 100% on my side, which is like, I just don't, I don't just, I just don't run in anymore. And I don't know if that's just like, experience, age, whatever, you know, like, I just, I just don't run in anymore like a puppy. It's just like, I've just accepted like she's an adult and she has things going on. And I ask him, like, are you in that state? And I also just know now certain times and I look at her schedule and see if it's open. And then I'm like, then I'll shoot the text. And then I'm like, much more likely to get what I want, which is her to just sit there and smile. Okay. How do you know what's a yes versus no? I think that's when what she just said applies first, which is like, let's look at the resources we have. You know, like, does this really make sense right now? Because right now we're struggling with just the things we have on our plate. Do you think adding more would, would, would help us get to where we're trying to go? Most times it's no. And I think that's part of your side as the, as the creator, innovator, that you have to, like, you have to separate the idea of like, I have ideas versus I have ideas that need to get done. And sometimes it's like, you need the emotional release of like, I have this thing, please listen to it. Just like there's some people who like, have a day that just happened, they really just want to tell you about it and don't want a solution. Right. So it's, it's like an equivalent for us is to just like, can I just tell you this thing and just say it's cool. Like we don't have to do it, but just like, isn't that awesome? And I think that that kind of like, qualms a lot of the emotional need that we have as creators to just share this creation we have without needing it. So just like separated in your own mind of like, is this, are we really going to change our business model? Because that's a big deal. You know what I mean? Okay. Can we say, so how do you not wear the other person out with your ideas, especially if they're not in the business? I would ask yourself if you're equally listening to them. So like, with the amount of time that you are, you are asking them to listen to you, are you spending equal amount of time listening to them about things that you might not, you might not care about? Final say for decision making, are all businesses just made together? Are there certain areas where each person has final say who has it? What if you don't agree? So for us, we operate from absolute trust. If Laila does make a decision without me, I absolutely do. I really genuinely, this isn't just like me saying this, like, not that I would say anything otherwise, but I genuinely trust if she makes a decision while I'm here that it's fine and it's probably just as good if not better than what I would have made. Big business decisions, like, I seek out her counsel. I'm like, what do you think about this? This is a big deal. Like, I would like her feedback. So she sees things from a totally different perspective than I do. And so we end up making much better decisions together. So like, I try and make as many decisions as possible with Laila. That's just my angle. If we don't agree, we don't move forward. So we don't move forward. That was actually a huge thing for us when we first got married. And it was someone told us once, they said, if you don't agree, don't move forward. And it really stuck with us. And because of that, I feel like we've made a lot better decisions. It was like the night after we got married at church, he was like, you don't agree, don't move forward. And I was like, we both like heard it. And we're like, okay. And so we pretty much just made that a rule. It's like, if you don't agree, it's like, we don't agree. It just means we're working on different data sets because we have the same values and we have the same goal. So it just means like, there's something that's not being communicated. There's something that's not being understood. So we just like keep digging until we figure, oh, that's how you're thinking about it. I understand. Cool. I like your way. Makes sense. And that's usually how it works. And then are there certain areas where each person is final say, for us, most things that are like sales marketing related, I will bounce off of her. But if there's like a, I think it might go this way. I'm like, I really feel like it's this way. She probably be like, I mean, that's kind of your thing. So I trust you. Same thing with like, anything leadership related, like anything running like the team and just being wise in general, I'd be like, you're pretty, you're better than I am at this. How often do we argue over what who wins? This is funny, who wins? Like, there's no winning arguments. So I don't think we argue that much at all. I would actually say that what many people would see is like, when we, when we're personal, like our personal talking about our relationship, our home life, that kind of stuff, if you were to like rip the two apart, we don't really have any arguments at all. But if it comes to business, we have productive, disruptive discussion, which is like, I think we should do this. And I'm like, dude, what the fuck, like we should do this. And then he's like, no, what are you talking about? Like if you look at X, Y and Z, and I'm like, what about this, this and this? And so like, it's, it's like that, but that's productive because we're trying to get to an end goal of like, we both agree on something, right? We're both confrontational. Now, some of you guys have a spouse slash other person who's in the business who is not confrontational. And like one of the hardest things in the world, if you're a steamroller, which I am, is to not steamroll. Because like, the person's opinion is just as good, if not better than yours and just as valid, they're just not as loud as you are. And so like learning when to just be like, what do you think? They're like, I don't know. And you're like, no, like for real, like, what do you think? Like, I know that this, like, you know, like you have to sometimes coax it out of someone until like, they're trained up to like, learn how to hit you in the head. How do you table it so it doesn't ruin the rest of the day? We don't move forward until we agree. Like if we had a fight this morning, if we had a fight, if we had a disagreement or something, like a fight in our world is like, you said something in a wrong, like that tone you just used, like what's going on? Like that felt off, what's up? And that's usually like, that's a fight in our world. It's like, what's going on? And then should be like, I don't know, you said something this morning about whatever. And I'm like, I did not know. I'm really sorry. I love you. You're beautiful. Like, let's be awesome. You know what I mean? And that's usually it. But we don't like, if that had happened this morning, we would have pushed the event back until it was right. I'm being dead serious. Like we would have pushed it back until it was right, because there's no point in getting in front of you guys until we're right. There's no point in of like, could you imagine us being in front of our team who relies on us as the rock, if they see instability in our relationship, it's like, is the business going to be here? Is there going to be like, long secrets and legacy secrets? Like is Layla secrets and Alice? You know, like what's going on? Like, like any tiny little crack is magnified in their minds because they're based on security. And so like, we have to appear like, I mean, we have to be rock for them. Alpha, with both strong personalities, how do you not get too strong with each other? I think for the guys in the room, I think you probably have a bunch to say about this, but for me, I think the best phrase in the world that has taught me so much about myself and has like, saved a lot of struggle is like, when you did this, I felt disrespected. And that's like, like, if you get angry at something your spouse does, it's usually because guys, we tend to have more pride and ego and anger comes from pride. It's from feeling like you deserve more better than what you just had. Right. I get annoyed when someone says good boy, because I feel like I'm better than a dog. Right. I'm working on it. But that's like, if you can trace down to that, usually when, you know, if Layla says something, I'll be like, I felt disrespectful. Like, I feel disrespected because I can feel the anger coming. I'm like, did you mean it that way? She's like, no, I'm like, okay, cool. Like, if you could say like this next time, that'd be great. But that's, that's all I got. Yeah. I mean, comparing who's better, I think is just like, that's not operating as a team. Because like, in our company, like, we would never be like, who's better? It's like, everyone's different. And so you're like, you and your spouse are not the same. You're each probably better at something than the other. There's no competition. And like, I don't know, I think that that's just a fundamental really poor point of view to look at a relationship. Let me piggyback on that, though, just because earlier on in our relationship, we both did the same things. And so until you have a separation of role that is going to come up. So it's like, does Alex takes the sales or does Layla take the sales? Because Layla's pretty good at sales, but Alex is kind of hot this week. Like, what's going on? But when we sold in the beginning, like, I could, I could understand where that would come from. Roles. All right, we'll be, we'll be as quick as we can on these. Okay, accountability. How to hold each other accountable? What if things don't get done? So that was a difficult one. Because we don't, to be really frank, we don't not do shit. I think it really comes down to, if so, let me take it from a different perspective. If somebody else like on the team, it doesn't get something done. The last thing I want to do is accuse them of like, what the heck you suck, like this didn't get done, like you didn't believe it. Like, I'm going to ask. And so if your spouse doesn't get something done, you would ask, like, did something else become a priority? Did something get in the way? Like, was it emotional? Was it logical? Like, what was the crux that didn't like allow you to get this done? And then saying, like, how can I help so you can get this done? And that we make sure this doesn't happen again. As simple as that. Just asking why. And usually it's me who doesn't do things. So Layla will be very kind and be like, did you do that thing that they need you to do? And I'll be like, oh, no. Maybe. I think the best thing in the world is waking up at 4am. I'm just telling you, like, I think the best thing in the world is waking up at 4am. Like no one bugs you until like eight or nine. It's like you get four, like four good hours every day before the day starts is like, you can, you can cure cancer. Miscellaneous. Okay. Last one values. Do you live by the Quartenance? Absolutely every day we even say it sometimes I'm like, babe, don't sugar coat it. Money. How do you manage money now and before you had it before your income increased? Believe it or not, we've always been big believers in we took every penny that we made and bought more information and coaching and training with it until our income exceeded what our lifestyle was. Not that we had a crazy lifestyle, but I didn't have a save, you know, a save penny saves a penny earned. I never really had that mindset. I always genuinely believed I would make enough to live the way I wanted to live. And it was like, if I'm making 60 grand a year now, I'm going to spend 60 because that's what I wear a mat. And but because I had full faith. So I wish I could tell you a sexier answer. Part of that is like, I mean, as as as unfortunate as this sounds, predicated on some level of self awareness, because as a general statement, it will take some people a lot longer to get to where they want to go than others. And in some instances, it's not responsible to do that. I just had a lot of conviction that I was going to get there. Just honest. Yeah, same Z's. Okay. Client boundaries. How do you identify shitty clients and protect yourselves from them? How do you know when to kick someone out? Can you ID them at the sale? You can ID them at the sale. But sometimes when you scale your sales and you have multiple sales people and they want to get commissions, you start selling yourself why this person is going to be a good fit. And you're like, I'm going to change this person's life. I'm going to change their outlook. And you don't. And they're just a dick. So you can probably ID them. But a lot of times you're just going to have to deal with it once you find out. How do you know when to kick something out? For us, if someone takes attention. So if someone takes attention once, it's like, it's like a three strike rule in my mind. It's like, once is first strike. And like, they're not aware of the strikes. Just like, if you take my attention three times, and it's negative each time, I'm going to kick you out because we don't need your money. We don't. And so it's just like, be nice. And if they're just being cruel to our team, for our team, if you're rude to our team, we'll just kick you out. Yeah, that's like no freaking. Yeah, you just have no tolerance. Well, it's especially when our team is nothing, but just like, I wish you could see the, because we hopped into one of our customer support meetings, and they made a presentation for one another about like how what they did impacts you guys and your members' lives, about how fixing the pixels and fixing the funnels changes lives. And I'm like, these people are doing everything in their power to help like Jim's work. And if you're addicted to them, like, come on. So how do you protect yourselves from them? We do kick them out and we say things like this. So just letting you know. So don't be mean. Final thoughts. Okay. Love and respect. So I think on the next one is resources. Those are probably three of the best resources that we have on running our relationship. But I'm going to go back one real quick. A lot of times people talk about love, loving your spouse. But I think what is really under talked about or under discussed in society is respect. And it goes both ways. A lot of times guys need respect and women need love. And so a lot of guys receive love from their spouses, but want respect. And they then go to other means to earn respect. And the way they do that is by scaring their wives so that they least respect them and they lose their love, because that's how more important it is to a guy to be respected. Being real. So wives respect your husbands. That's, there's some truth. There's a book that talks about that. And husbands love your wives. So, but it goes both ways because like there are some women, I would argue Layla probably values respect more than love. Not that they're not both important, you need both. But when asked, if you like, if you take a test, you can see where people fall on a scale. So if you ever feel like you get angry, it's usually because of the respect one is a guy. I'm going to talk about the second one. About trust and loyalty. You put that on there. Okay. I didn't know what it meant. I was like, I don't know. Yeah, trust and loyalty is absolute. And so basically, like if you have like trust issues with your spouse, like you can't, you can't move forward from that. Like that needs to be handled before anything else can be talked about because like that's, that's like the first level is like, do I trust you? If I don't trust you, then there's literally nowhere we can go from here. Like we can't have a business together. We can't have a family together because nothing you say, I believe, which means you can't have basic communication. And so trust is the first thing that happens in basic communication. Right. So that's a communication cycle that breaks. Like that has to be first priority. So if you don't trust your spouse, address it. Like there's literally nothing else I can do for you for the rest of the summit, go address it. And if you fucked up, own it and move forward. And loyalty is something that's earned from trust from a long period of time. Do you want to talk about the last one? Yeah. I mean, really just going all in once the agreement is made. So for us, we felt like once we got married, it, what we were more fearful of making our relationship amazing because we're like, there's no way out. Right. And so we went all in in the beginning and read all the books that we could. We got like, I think we ordered like, I don't even know, like 25 off of the Amazon, we're just like reading everything like all the divorce books and all the like, you know, post divorce Trump, because we're like, what are these people trying to tell others of like where they messed up? Right. And then we're like, let's not do that. Right. And so that's why we share the step with you guys, because really once you go in and you get married, a lot of people think that you just need love. But in reality, it's a lot of work. And so we like to compare it to like, it's like a business, honestly, like you have to put work into it every day. And it's not always going to be easy. Like there's many days where I'm like, it's right, like marriage is hard, like it is work, like I have to consciously, we consciously have to come to agreements, we consciously have to be aware of each other's thoughts. Like we have to put a lot of work for those things. I haven't felt like it was that much work. I'm just being really honest. It's been pretty chill. She's like, I'm going to love Alex right now. I was like, she's great. He was awesome. I love Layla. Well, I think managing your state for another person is really important, right? Taking care of yourself for another person as well. So just going all in once that agreement is made. Okay, cool. Those are the resources and the wish list. As we referenced earlier, I highly recommend it. If there's one thing that you do from this presentation is define your ideal scene with your spouse and actually do it. And then you'll be amazed how quickly things can transform.
